docs.title: Table/List Steps
docs.text: Gherkin Step Intent Then the table should have {count} rows Row count Then the table should contain {text} in column {column} Cell content When I sort the table by {column} Column sort When I filter by {column} with {value} Table filter When I click on row {index} Row selection

docs.title: Dialog/Modal Steps
docs.text: Gherkin Step Intent Then I should see a dialog with title {title} Dialog present When I confirm the dialog Accept dialog When I dismiss the dialog Cancel dialog When I fill in the dialog field {field} with {value} Dialog input

docs.title: Locator Resolution Strategy
docs.text:
Each helper function must resolve field/element names to Playwright locators using this priority:

function resolveLocator(page: Page, name: string): Locator {
  // 1. Try by label (most accessible)
  const byLabel = page.getByLabel(name);
  // 2. Try by role with name
  const byRole = page.getByRole('textbox', { name });
  // 3. Try by placeholder
  const byPlaceholder = page.getByPlaceholder(name);
  // 4. Try by test-id (kebab-case conversion)
  const testId = name.toLowerCase().replace(/\s+/g, '-');
  const byTestId = page.getByTestId(testId);
  // Return first visible match
  return byLabel.or(byRole).or(byPlaceholder).or(byTestId);
}

docs.title: Adding Custom Vocabulary
docs.text:
Teams can extend with domain-specific steps:

When I approve the order for {customer}
Then the invoice should be generated
When I assign the case to {agent}

Rules: 1. Must follow the same naming patterns (declarative, business language) 2. Must have helper functions in all supported languages 3. Must be documented in the step mapping table 4. Must not duplicate existing vocabulary steps

docs.title: Norwegian Vocabulary (Parallel)
docs.text:
# language: no

# Navigasjon
Gitt at jeg er på {side}-siden
Gitt at jeg er innlogget som {rolle}
Når jeg navigerer til {side}

# Skjema
Når jeg fyller inn {felt} med {verdi}
Når jeg velger {alternativ} fra {nedtrekksliste}
Når jeg huker av {avkryssingsboks}
Når jeg sender inn skjemaet

# Påstander
Så skal jeg se {tekst}
Så skal jeg ikke se {tekst}
Så skal feltet {felt} ha verdien {verdi}
Så skal feltet {felt} være tomt
Så skal {element} være synlig
Så skal {element} være skjult
Så skal jeg se en bekreftelsesmelding
Så skal jeg se en feilmelding

View on GitHub Download SKILL.md

Runs stable, fast browser E2E test suites with eventual-consistency primitives and CI sharding guidance. Language-agnostic architecture supporting JS/TS, Python, and C# adapters.

docs.title: Set up E2E suite
docs.text: Set up a structured Playwright E2E test suite with eventual consistency support for our event-sourced backend.

docs.title: Configure CI sharding

docs.title: Define test flows
docs.text: Create suite and flow YAML specs for the checkout workflow.

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Playwright E2E Runner
docs.text: Run stable, fast browser E2E suites against a running frontend for greenfield and brownfield systems.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• You need reliable E2E validation in CI/CD
  • Event-sourced/eventually-consistent backends make fixed sleeps flaky
  • You need sharding and merged reporting to reduce wall-clock duration
  • You support multiple adapter languages (JS/TS, Python, C#)

Use playwright for quick ad-hoc scripts. Use this skill for structured suites.

docs.title: Architecture (language-agnostic)
docs.text:
Keep three layers separate: 1. Spec layer (YAML): suite/flow behavior definitions 2. Intent layer: shared step semantics (navigate, act, assert, eventually) 3. Adapter layer: thin language-specific implementations

Recommended folders:

  • qa/e2e/suites/ — suite specs (*.suite.yml)
  • qa/e2e/flows/ — flow specs (*.flow.yml)
  • qa/e2e/environments/ — local/ci/staging env templates
  • qa/e2e/fixtures/ — seeds/test data contracts
  • qa/e2e/snapshots/ — ARIA/snapshot baselines
  • qa/outputs/reports|traces|videos|screenshots/ — generated artifacts

docs.title: Core reliability rule: eventually everywhere
docs.text:
Never rely on fixed sleep for domain propagation.

Use shared primitive:

eventually(assertion, timeout=60s, intervals=[1s, 2s, 10s])

Adapter mapping: - JS/TS: expect.poll / expect().toPass() - Python: polling helper wrapping Playwright assertions - C#: retry helper with bounded timeout and interval schedule

docs.title: Locator strategy standard
docs.text:
1. getByTestId() (preferred)
  2. getByRole() (+ accessible name)
  3. getByLabel() / getByText()
  4. CSS/XPath only as last resort

docs.title: Auth and artifact policy
docs.text:
• Reuse login state via playwright/.auth/ (gitignored)
  • Trace: on-first-retry
  • Video: on-first-retry
  • Screenshot: on failure

docs.title: CI strategy
docs.text:
Primary engine: Playwright Test.

  • Shard test run: --shard=x/y
  • Use blob/merge reporting for deterministic summary in parallel pipelines
  • Keep smoke suite for PR fast-feedback; full suite in heavier CI stage
  • Works for both GitHub Actions and TeamCity patterns

docs.title: Docker guidance
docs.text:
• Use official Playwright image as runtime base
  • Add project dependencies and adapter runtime tooling
  • Keep browser deps pinned per project lock strategy

docs.title: Completion checklist
docs.text:
• Specs defined (suite.yml + flow.yml)
  • Eventual consistency paths covered by eventually
  • Auth state reuse configured
  • CI sharding + report merge configured
  • Failure artifacts retained
  • No fixed sleeps in critical assertions

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Playwright Ops (Reusable Browser Operations)
docs.text: Build reusable browser operations that can run from prompts/CLI macros and from maintainable code adapters.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• Repeating admin/data-entry workflows
  • Evidence capture for support/compliance
  • Runbook automations that must be deterministic

Use this skill when one-off scripts become repeated operational tasks.

docs.title: Operation contract
docs.text:
Define operations as declarative specs (*.op.yml) with explicit inputs/outputs/artifacts.

Example schema:

name: create-user
inputs:
  email: string
  role: string
steps:
  - navigate: /admin/users
  - action: click
    target: testid:new-user
  - action: fill
    target: testid:email
    value: ${email}
outputs:
  user_id: string
artifacts:
  trace: on-first-retry
  screenshot: on-failure

docs.title: Dual execution surfaces
docs.text:
1. CLI macros (fast path): quick operational execution and triage
  2. Code adapters (maintainable path): Page Objects/helpers per language

Both should consume the same operation intent, not duplicate behavior.

docs.title: Brownfield scalability guidance
docs.text:
• Keep selectors centralized (libs/selectors/)
  • Encapsulate page behaviors in Page Objects
  • Keep operation specs small and composable
  • Favor composition over giant monolithic flows

docs.title: Eventual consistency and verification
docs.text:
Ops often trigger async backend work. Add post-action verification with eventual checks:

eventually(assertion, timeout=60s, intervals=[1s,2s,10s])

Prefer API-assisted verification when UI propagation is delayed.

docs.title: Evidence bundle policy
docs.text:
On operation failures, capture: - Trace - Screenshot - Snapshot/DOM capture - Structured operation input/output metadata

Store outputs under qa/outputs/ and keep generated artifacts gitignored.

docs.title: Security and safety
docs.text:
• Mask secrets in logs/artifacts
  • Use least-privilege operation accounts
  • Keep destructive ops behind explicit confirmations
  • Require idempotency notes for operations that may be retried

docs.title: Completion checklist
docs.text:
• *.op.yml operation spec created
  • Adapter mapping exists for target language(s)
  • Eventual consistency checks included
  • Evidence artifacts configured
  • Inputs/outputs documented
  • CI task includes at least one replay/verification job

Record browser navigation as versioned artifacts and validate replay in CI with snapshot-aware checks.

Captures browser navigation as versioned artifacts and validates replay in CI with snapshot-aware checks. Enables record-now, validate-later workflows for UI flow verification.

docs.text: Record the login → dashboard → settings flow as a versioned recording bundle.

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Playwright Recorder (Record & Validate)
docs.text: Capture durable browser navigation artifacts, version them, and validate them later in CI.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• Teams manually discover UI flows and need reproducible evidence
  • You want record-now, validate-later workflows
  • You need change-aware replay checks in pull requests and nightlies

Primary recording surface can be playwright-cli (interactive), while validation is handled by adapter tests.

docs.title: Recording bundle format
docs.text:
Store one bundle per flow:

  • flow.yml — semantic flow metadata + checkpoints
  • snapshots/ — ARIA/DOM snapshots (structured, not pixel-only)
  • traces/ — Playwright traces for failure analysis
  • metadata.json — app version, env, author, timestamps, tags

Suggested location: qa/e2e/recordings/<flow-name>/

docs.title: Record workflow
docs.text:
1. Open target app/session
  2. Capture baseline snapshot
  3. Perform interaction step
  4. Re-snapshot after each mutation/navigation
  5. Add explicit checkpoints
  6. Save bundle + metadata

docs.title: Validation workflow
docs.text:
In CI:

  1. Load recording bundle
  2. Replay steps with environment-specific config
  3. Run structural validation (ARIA/snapshot assertions)
  4. Apply eventual consistency checkpoints where projections lag
  5. Persist artifacts on mismatch

docs.title: Eventual consistency support
docs.text:
Use checkpoint entries in flow.yml:

checkpoints:
  - id: order-visible
    type: eventually
    timeout: 60s
    intervals: [1s, 2s, 10s]

docs.title: Gherkin integration (optional)
docs.text: If Gherkin exists, treat feature/scenario as source intent and materialize recording bundles from it. If not, YAML flow remains first-class.

docs.title: CI policy
docs.text:
• PR: replay critical recordings (smoke)
  • Nightly: replay full bundle set
  • Keep traces/screenshots for failed validations
  • Use sharding for large recording suites

docs.title: Guardrails
docs.text:
• Never store secrets in recording metadata
  • Keep auth state externalized and gitignored
  • Avoid brittle CSS-only selectors in recording checkpoints
  • Prefer semantic assertions over pixel-only checks

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Playwright Browser Automation
docs.text: General-purpose browser automation skill. Write custom Playwright code for any automation task and execute it.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Testing web pages, forms, login flows
  • Taking screenshots (single or responsive multi-viewport)
  • Checking for broken links
  • Visual regression testing
  • Validating web functionality
  • Automating any browser-based interaction
  • E2E testing during workflows

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js installed
  • Playwright installed: npm install playwright (or npx playwright install)
  • Chromium browser: npx playwright install chromium

docs.title: Quick Setup
docs.text:
npm install playwright
npx playwright install chromium

docs.title: Critical Workflow
docs.text: Follow these steps in order:

docs.title: 1. Auto-Detect Dev Servers (for localhost testing)
docs.text:
ALWAYS detect running dev servers FIRST before writing test code:

# Check for running dev servers on common ports
for port in 3000 3001 4200 5000 5173 8000 8080 8888; do
  (echo >/dev/tcp/localhost/$port) 2>/dev/null && echo "Server on port $port"
done
  • If 1 server found → use it automatically, inform user
  • If multiple servers found → ask user which one to test
  • If no servers found → ask for URL or offer to help start dev server

docs.title: 2. Write Scripts to /tmp
docs.text: NEVER write test files to the project directory. Always use /tmp/playwright-test-*.js (or OS temp equivalent).

docs.title: 3. Use Visible Browser by Default
docs.text: Always use headless: false unless user specifically requests headless mode.

docs.title: 4. Parameterize URLs
docs.text: Always make URLs configurable via a TARGET_URL constant at the top of every script.

docs.title: Execution Pattern
docs.text:
// /tmp/playwright-test-page.js
const { chromium } = require('playwright');

const TARGET_URL = 'http://localhost:3001'; // Auto-detected or from user

(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false });
  const page = await browser.newPage();

  await page.goto(TARGET_URL);
  console.log('Page loaded:', await page.title());

  await page.screenshot({ path: '/tmp/screenshot.png', fullPage: true });
  console.log('📸 Screenshot saved to /tmp/screenshot.png');

  await browser.close();
})();

Execute: 

node /tmp/playwright-test-page.js

docs.title: Test a Page (Multiple Viewports)
docs.text:
const { chromium } = require('playwright');
const TARGET_URL = 'http://localhost:3001';

(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false, slowMo: 100 });
  const page = await browser.newPage();

  // Desktop
  await page.setViewportSize({ width: 1920, height: 1080 });
  await page.goto(TARGET_URL);
  await page.screenshot({ path: '/tmp/desktop.png', fullPage: true });

  // Tablet
  await page.setViewportSize({ width: 768, height: 1024 });
  await page.screenshot({ path: '/tmp/tablet.png', fullPage: true });

  // Mobile
  await page.setViewportSize({ width: 375, height: 667 });
  await page.screenshot({ path: '/tmp/mobile.png', fullPage: true });

  console.log('✅ All viewports tested');
  await browser.close();
})();

docs.title: Test Login Flow
docs.text:
const { chromium } = require('playwright');
const TARGET_URL = 'http://localhost:3001';

(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false });
  const page = await browser.newPage();

  await page.goto(`${TARGET_URL}/login`);
  await page.fill('input[name="email"]', 'test@example.com');
  await page.fill('input[name="password"]', 'password123');
  await page.click('button[type="submit"]');

  await page.waitForURL('**/dashboard');
  console.log('✅ Login successful, redirected to dashboard');
  await browser.close();
})();

docs.title: Fill and Submit Form
docs.text:
const { chromium } = require('playwright');
const TARGET_URL = 'http://localhost:3001';

(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false, slowMo: 50 });
  const page = await browser.newPage();

  await page.goto(`${TARGET_URL}/contact`);
  await page.fill('input[name="name"]', 'John Doe');
  await page.fill('input[name="email"]', 'john@example.com');
  await page.fill('textarea[name="message"]', 'Test message');
  await page.click('button[type="submit"]');

  await page.waitForSelector('.success-message');
  console.log('✅ Form submitted successfully');
  await browser.close();
})();

docs.title: Check for Broken Links
docs.text:
const { chromium } = require('playwright');

(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false });
  const page = await browser.newPage();

  await page.goto('http://localhost:3000');
  const links = await page.locator('a[href^="http"]').all();
  const results = { working: 0, broken: [] };

  for (const link of links) {
    const href = await link.getAttribute('href');
    try {
      const response = await page.request.head(href);
      if (response.ok()) results.working++;
      else results.broken.push({ url: href, status: response.status() });
    } catch (e) {
      results.broken.push({ url: href, error: e.message });
    }
  }

  console.log(`✅ Working links: ${results.working}`);
  console.log(`❌ Broken links:`, results.broken);
  await browser.close();
})();

docs.title: Visual Regression Testing
docs.text:
const { chromium } = require('playwright');
const TARGET_URL = 'http://localhost:3001';

(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false });
  const page = await browser.newPage();

  await page.goto(TARGET_URL);
  await page.waitForLoadState('networkidle');

  // Take baseline screenshot
  await page.screenshot({
    path: '/tmp/visual-regression.png',
    fullPage: true,
  });

  console.log('📸 Baseline screenshot saved');
  console.log('Compare with previous baseline to detect regressions');
  await browser.close();
})();

docs.title: Inline Execution (Simple Tasks)
docs.text:
For quick one-off tasks, execute code inline:

node -e "
const { chromium } = require('playwright');
(async () => {
  const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false });
  const page = await browser.newPage();
  await page.goto('http://localhost:3001');
  await page.screenshot({ path: '/tmp/quick-screenshot.png', fullPage: true });
  console.log('Screenshot saved');
  await browser.close();
})();
"

When to use inline vs files: - Inline: Quick one-off tasks (screenshot, check element, get page title) - Files: Complex tests, responsive checks, anything user might want to re-run

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• CRITICAL: Detect servers FIRST — always check for running dev servers before writing test code
  • Use /tmp for test files — write to /tmp/playwright-test-*.js, never to project directory
  • Parameterize URLs — put detected/provided URL in a TARGET_URL constant at top of every script
  • DEFAULT: Visible browser — always use headless: false unless user explicitly asks for headless
  • Slow down: Use slowMo: 100 to make actions visible
  • Wait strategies: Use waitForURL, waitForSelector, waitForLoadState instead of fixed timeouts
  • Error handling: Always use try-catch for robust automation
  • Console output: Use console.log() to track progress

docs.title: Integration with
docs.text: When invoked from : 1. Write Playwright E2E tests that complement unit/integration tests 2. Focus on user-facing workflows (login, forms, navigation) 3. Report results back for test coverage analysis 4. Create issues for any broken functionality found

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Playwright not installed: npm install playwright && npx playwright install chromium
  • Module not found: Ensure Playwright is installed globally or in the project
  • Browser doesn't open: Check headless: false and ensure display is available
  • Element not found: Add wait: await page.waitForSelector('.element', { timeout: 10000 })

docs.title: Systematic Debugging
docs.text:
Systematic debugging approaches for isolating and fixing software defects. Use when something isn't working and the cause is unclear.

utility

View on GitHub Download SKILL.md

Provides systematic debugging approaches for isolating and fixing software defects. Use when something isn't working and the cause is unclear — follows structured investigation rather than guesswork.

docs.title: Debug a failing test
docs.text: This test in tests/test_auth.py is failing intermittently — help me debug it systematically.

docs.title: Isolate a production error
docs.text: Users are seeing 500 errors on the /api/orders endpoint — help me trace the root cause.

docs.title: Fix a regression
docs.text: Something broke after the last merge — the login flow redirects to a blank page.

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Skill: debugging
docs.text: Systematic debugging approaches for isolating and fixing software defects

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Systematic problem isolation, root cause analysis, and defect resolution. Use when something isn't working and the cause is unclear.

docs.title: 1. Understand Before Acting
docs.text:
• Reproduce the issue: Can you consistently trigger the problem?
  • Define expected vs actual: What should happen vs what is happening?
  • Gather context: When does this occur? Under what conditions?
  • Recent changes: What changed before this appeared?

docs.title: 2. Isolate the Problem
docs.text:
• Binary search: Comment out half the code, test, repeat
  • Minimize reproduction: Create minimal test case
  • Control variables: Change one thing at a time
  • Eliminate noise: Remove unrelated factors

docs.title: 3. Form Hypotheses
docs.text:
• State your assumption: "I believe X is causing Y because..."
  • Make predictions: "If my hypothesis is true, then Z should happen"
  • Test predictions: Verify or refute each hypothesis
  • Iterate: Refine hypothesis based on evidence

docs.title: 4. Fix and Verify
docs.text:
• Address root cause: Not just symptoms
  • Minimize changes: Smallest fix that resolves the issue
  • Add tests: Prevent regression
  • Verify fix: Test the specific scenario and related scenarios

docs.title: Systematic Debugging Process
docs.text: Credit: This 4-phase process is adapted from obra/superpowers by Jesse Vincent (MIT License). See project docs Section 4 and obra/superpowers skills/systematic-debugging/SKILL.md.

docs.title: Phase 1: Root Cause Investigation
docs.text:
1. Check existing lessons on this issue (FIRST)
  2. Run to see if prior debugging attempts left clues
  3. Prior lessons often contain root cause hints or dead-end paths to avoid

  4. Also check lessons on related issues in the same epic

  5. Read error messages carefully (don't skip or skim)

  6. What does the error actually say?
  7. Stack trace analysis - where did it fail?

  8. Error codes and their meanings

  9. Reproduce the issue consistently

  10. Can you trigger the problem reliably?
  11. Document exact reproduction steps

  12. Identify minimal reproduction case

  13. Check recent changes

  14. git diff to see what changed
  15. When did this start happening?

  16. Correlate with recent commits

  17. Gather evidence at component boundaries

  18. Input vs output at each boundary
  19. HTTP request/response logs
  20. Database queries and results

  21. API contract violations

  22. Trace data flow backward

  23. Start from the error point
  24. Work backwards through the call stack
  25. Identify where data diverges from expected

docs.title: Phase 2: Pattern Analysis
docs.text:
1. Find working examples in codebase
  2. Where does similar code work?
  3. What's different about the failing case?

  4. Copy working patterns

  5. Compare against references

  6. Documentation examples
  7. Library/API reference

  8. Stack Overflow/issue trackers

  9. Identify differences

  10. Configuration differences
  11. Data type mismatches
  12. Missing initialization

  13. API version differences

  14. Understand dependencies

  15. What does this code depend on?
  16. Are dependencies satisfied?
  17. Version compatibility issues

docs.title: Phase 3: Hypothesis and Testing
docs.text:
1. Form single, specific hypothesis
  2. "I believe X is causing Y because Z"
  3. Make it falsifiable - what would prove it wrong?

  4. Avoid multiple explanations - pick one

  5. Test with minimal change

  6. Change ONE thing at a time
  7. Verify hypothesis with logging or instrumentation

  8. Don't implement full fix yet

  9. Verify before continuing

  10. Did the test confirm or refute your hypothesis?
  11. If refuted, form new hypothesis
  12. If confirmed, proceed to Phase 4

docs.title: Phase 4: Implementation
docs.text:
1. Create failing test that reproduces bug (MANDATORY)
  2. Test must fail for the exact bug being fixed
  3. Run it and confirm it fails

  4. This proves you're testing the right thing

  5. Implement single fix for root cause

  6. Address the root cause, not symptoms
  7. Minimal change that fixes the bug

  8. Don't add unnecessary complexity

  9. Verify fix works

  10. Failing test now passes
  11. No regressions introduced

  12. Run full test suite if available

  13. If 3+ fixes fail → question architecture

  14. If you've tried 3+ fixes and none work:
  15. The architecture or design may be the problem
  16. Stop and ask: "Should we reconsider the approach?"

docs.title: "It Works on My Machine"
docs.text: Check Action Environment differences Python versions, OS, dependencies Uncommitted config Local settings, .env files Race conditions Timing-dependent issues Data differences Test with production data subset Resource constraints Production may have different limits

docs.title: Intermittent Failures
docs.text: Check Action Shared state Global variables, singletons, caches Timing Race conditions, timeouts, async issues Randomness Random seeds, shuffling, sampling Resource cleanup Are resources properly released? External dependencies Network calls, third-party services

docs.title: Performance Degradation
docs.text: Check Action Profile first Measure before optimizing O(n²) Nested loops, repeated work I/O Database queries, file reads, network Memory Leaks, large objects, excessive allocations Caching Repeated expensive operations

docs.title: Memory Leaks
docs.text: Check Action Profile memory Track allocations over time Circular references GC can't collect cycles Event listeners Detached handlers keeping objects alive Caches Growing without bounds Static collections Accumulating entries

docs.title: Deadlocks
docs.text: Check Action Lock order Identify held locks, acquisition order Cycles A waits for B, B waits for A Timeouts Are operations waiting indefinitely? Hold-and-wait Holding one lock while waiting for another

docs.title: Print/Log Statements
docs.text:
# Strategic placement with unique markers
print(f"[DEBUG-001] user_id={user_id}, state={state}")

# Include enough context
logger.debug(f"Processing item {i}/{total}: {item.id}")

# Remove after debugging!

docs.title: Debugger
docs.text:
• Set breakpoints at suspicious locations, not everywhere
  • Watch expressions for specific variables
  • Check call stack to understand how you got here
  • Step carefully through suspicious code

docs.title: Tests for Debugging
docs.text:
• Write failing test that captures bug reproduction
  • Use git bisect to find when bug was introduced
  • Mock external dependencies to isolate
  • Property-based testing finds edge cases

docs.title: Anti-Patterns to Avoid
docs.text: Anti-Pattern Problem Better Approach Shotgun debugging Random changes hoping something works Form hypothesis, test, refine Symptom treatment Adding error handling to hide failures Fix underlying cause Assuming "This variable can't be null" Add assertion to verify Overcomplicating Complex debugging infrastructure Start simple, add tools as needed Ignoring evidence Dismissing data that doesn't fit Revise hypothesis to explain all

docs.title: Debugging Checklist
docs.text:
Before declaring "debugged":

  • Root cause identified, not just symptom treated
  • Failing test created and confirmed to fail (MANDATORY)
  • Single fix implemented for root cause
  • Fix verified - failing test now passes
  • No regressions (run full test suite if available)
  • Related code checked for same issue
  • Documentation updated if needed
  • Fix verified in realistic scenario

docs.title: Fix Attempts Tracking (MANDATORY)
docs.text:
Track fix attempts to detect architectural issues:

## Debug Session: [Issue Description]

**Fix Attempt 1**: [Date]
- Hypothesis: [Your theory]
- Fix: [What you tried]
- Result: [Pass/Fail]

**Fix Attempt 2**: [Date]
- Hypothesis: [Refined theory]
- Fix: [What you tried]
- Result: [Pass/Fail]

**Fix Attempt 3**: [Date]
- Hypothesis: [Further refinement]
- Fix: [What you tried]
- Result: [Pass/Fail]

⚠️ 3+ FIX ATTEMPTS FAILED

Consider:
- Is the architecture or design the problem?
- Should we question assumptions?
- Stop and ask: "Should we reconsider the approach?"

Rule: If 3+ fix attempts fail, STOP and question architecture before continuing.

docs.title: When to Escalate
docs.text:
Consider asking for help if:

  • After 2 hours without progress
  • Issue is in unfamiliar technology stack
  • Problem involves complex distributed systems
  • Security implications
  • Production outage
  • Going in circles (revisiting same hypotheses)

docs.title: Recording Debug Sessions
docs.text:
Track in project docs:

## Debugging: [Issue Description]

**Symptom**: [What's happening]
**Expected**: [What should happen]
**Reproduction**: [Steps to trigger]

### Hypotheses
1. [Hypothesis] → [TESTED: result]
2. [Hypothesis] → [PENDING]

### Evidence Gathered
- Log at X showed Y
- Variable Z had value W

### Resolution
[Root cause and fix applied]

docs.title: Markdown to Jira Conversion
docs.text:
Convert standard markdown syntax into Jira-compatible wiki markup

utility

View on GitHub Download SKILL.md

Converts standard markdown syntax into Jira-compatible wiki markup. Handles headings, bold/italic, code blocks, links, tables, nested lists, and other formatting differences.

docs.title: Convert a README
docs.text: Convert this README.md to Jira markup so I can paste it into a Jira issue.

docs.title: Convert release notes
docs.text: Convert CHANGELOG.md to Jira format for the release issue.

docs.title: Quick conversion
docs.text:
Convert this markdown text to Jira markup:

## Features
- **Bold item**
- `code item`

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Convert standard markdown files or text into Jira-compatible wiki markup. Jira uses a proprietary markup syntax that differs from standard markdown, requiring translation for content pasted into Jira issues, comments, or documentation.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify input: Accept a file path to a markdown file
  2. Read content: Load the markdown content
  3. Apply conversions: Transform markdown syntax to Jira markup (see conversion table below)
  4. Output result: Display the converted Jira markup for user to copy

docs.title: Conversion Reference
docs.text: Markdown Jira Markup Notes # Heading 1 h1. Heading 1 Headers h1-h6 ## Heading 2 h2. Heading 2 **bold** *bold* Bold text *italic* or _italic_ _italic_ Italic text ~~strikethrough~~ -strikethrough- Strikethrough `inline code` {{inline code}} Inline code ```lang code ``` {code:lang} code {code} Code blocks [text](url) [text\|url] Links ![alt](url) !url! Images - item or * item * item Bullet list 1. item # item Numbered list > quote {quote}text{quote} Blockquotes --- or *** ---- Horizontal rule | a | b | \|\| a \|\| b \|\| Table headers | 1 | 2 | \| 1 \| 2 \| Table rows

docs.title: Nested List Handling
docs.text:
Markdown nested lists use indentation. Jira uses repeated markers:

Markdown: 

- Item 1
  - Nested 1
    - Deep nested
- Item 2

Jira: 

* Item 1
** Nested 1
*** Deep nested
* Item 2

Numbered nested: 

# Item 1
## Nested 1
### Deep nested
# Item 2

docs.title: Code Block Languages
docs.text:
Common mappings for syntax highlighting:

Markdown Jira ```javascript {code:javascript} ```python {code:python} ```java {code:java} csharp orcs {code:c#} ```sql {code:sql} bash orshell {code:bash} ```json {code:json} ```xml {code:xml} ```yaml {code:yaml} (no language) {code}

docs.title: Multi-line Blockquotes
docs.text:
Markdown blockquotes span multiple > prefixed lines. In Jira, wrap entire content:

Markdown: 

> Line 1
> Line 2
> Line 3

Jira: 

{quote}
Line 1
Line 2
Line 3
{quote}

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
Jira requires || for header cells and | for data cells:

Markdown: 

| Name | Value |
|------|-------|
| foo  | 123   |
| bar  | 456   |

Jira: 

|| Name || Value ||
| foo | 123 |
| bar | 456 |

docs.title: Escaping Pipe Characters
docs.text: Pipes inside table cells: use \| in both formats.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Input markdown file read successfully
  • All markdown syntax converted to Jira equivalents
  • Output displayed for user copying
  • No unconverted markdown syntax remaining

docs.title: Anti-patterns (avoid)
docs.text:
• ❌ Modifying the original markdown file
  • ❌ Attempting to convert HTML embedded in markdown (pass through as-is)
  • ❌ Guessing at markdown syntax not covered by conversion table
  • ❌ Creating output files without user request (display only by default)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Input (README.md): 

# Project Title

This is **important** and _emphasized_.

## Features

- Feature 1
- Feature 2
  - Sub-feature

```python
def hello():
    print("world")

Documentation 

**Output** (Jira markup):
h1. Project Title

This is important and emphasized.

h2. Features

  • Feature 1
  • Feature 2 ** Sub-feature

{code:python} def hello(): print("world")

[Documentation|https://example.com] ```

docs.title: Deep Topic Research
docs.text:
Deep topic research with optional issue creation from findings. Use for researching technologies, patterns, libraries, or any topic requiring investigation.

utility

View on GitHub Download SKILL.md

Conducts deep topic research with structured output. Supports quick, deep, and comparative research modes for investigating technologies, patterns, libraries, or any topic requiring investigation.

docs.title: Research a technology
docs.text: Do a deep research on CQRS + Event Sourcing patterns for Python applications.

docs.title: Compare alternatives
docs.text: Compare FastAPI vs Litestar vs Starlette for our API backend — comparative research.

docs.title: Quick investigation
docs.text: Quick research: what are the current best practices for Python dependency management?

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Conduct structured research on topics, technologies, libraries, patterns, or any subject requiring investigation. Produces documented findings with optional issue creation for actionable items.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Evaluating a new technology or library
  • Investigating best practices for a pattern
  • Researching solutions to a problem
  • Comparing alternatives (frameworks, tools, approaches)
  • Understanding external APIs or services
  • Preparing for a design decision

docs.title: Quick Research (default)
docs.text:
Fast investigation using available tools and knowledge.

/research "FastAPI vs Flask for REST APIs"
→ Quick comparison based on docs and knowledge

docs.title: Deep Research
docs.text:
Thorough investigation with documentation lookup, code analysis, and structured output.

/research deep "Implementing OAuth2 in Python"
→ Detailed findings with examples and recommendations

docs.title: Comparative Research
docs.text:
Side-by-side evaluation of alternatives.

/research compare "pytest vs unittest vs nose2"
→ Feature matrix, pros/cons, recommendation

docs.title: 1. Scope Definition
docs.text:
Before researching, clarify: - Topic: What exactly are we researching? - Context: Why do we need this information? - Constraints: Time budget, depth required, specific questions? - Output: What format is most useful?

If scope is unclear, invoke interview for one question at a time.

docs.title: 2. Information Gathering
docs.text:
Sources (in priority order):

  1. Workspace Context
  2. Existing code patterns
  3. Project documentation
  4. Constitution constraints

  5. Previous research (project docs)

  6. Built-in Knowledge

  7. Language/framework documentation
  8. Common patterns and best practices

  9. Known tradeoffs and gotchas

  10. External Tools (if available)

  11. Web search via MCP tools
  12. Documentation lookup

  13. API exploration

  14. Code Analysis

  15. Read relevant source code
  16. Analyze existing implementations
  17. Check test patterns

docs.title: 3. Synthesis
docs.text: Organize findings into: - Summary: Key takeaways (1-3 sentences) - Details: Structured findings - Recommendations: What to do based on findings - Questions: What remains unclear - References: Sources used

docs.title: 4. Output
docs.text:
Console Output (default): 

## Research: {topic}

### Summary
{1-3 sentence summary}

### Findings
{detailed structured findings}

### Recommendations
{actionable recommendations}

### Open Questions
{what we still don't know}

File Output (with --save or for deep research): - Location: project docs - Includes full details, references, examples

docs.title: Technology Evaluation
docs.text:
## Research: {Technology Name}

### Summary
{What it is and whether we should use it}

### Overview
- **What**: {description}
- **Use case**: {when to use}
- **Alternatives**: {competing solutions}

### Evaluation

| Criterion | Rating | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Maturity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Active development, 5+ years |

### Pros

docs.title: Comparative Analysis
docs.text:
## Research: {Option A} vs {Option B} vs {Option C}

### Summary
{which is best for our use case and why}

### Feature Matrix

| Feature | Option A | Option B | Option C |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| {feature 1} | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| {feature 2} | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| {feature 3} | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ |

### Detailed Comparison

#### Option A
- **Strengths**: ...

- **Best for**: ...

#### Option B
...

### Recommendation
{recommendation with rationale}

docs.title: Problem Investigation
docs.text:
## Research: {Problem Description}

### Summary
{root cause and solution}

### Problem Statement
{clear description of the problem}

### Investigation

#### Hypothesis 1: {hypothesis}
- **Evidence for**: ...

docs.title: Issue Creation from Research
docs.text:
After research, if actionable items are found:

📋 Research complete. Found {N} actionable items:

- [FEAT] Implement caching layer (from performance findings)
- [CHORE] Update dependency to fix vulnerability
- [DOCS] Document new API integration

Create issues for these? [A]ll / [S]elect / [N]one

docs.title: Integration with Other Skills
docs.text:
• ****: Research can be triggered from idea enrichment
  • ****: Planning can request research for unknowns
  • ****: Research findings become backlog items
  • interview: Used to clarify research scope

docs.title: MCP Tool Integration (Optional)
docs.text:
If MCP tools are available, research can use:

Tool Use Case web_search Search for documentation, tutorials, comparisons fetch_url Retrieve specific documentation pages brave_search Alternative web search

Without MCP tools: Research uses built-in knowledge and workspace analysis only.

docs.title: Research Confidence
docs.text:
Rate research confidence based on sources:

Confidence Sources High Official docs + code analysis + personal experience Medium Official docs OR established best practices Low Community opinions, limited sources, outdated info

Always state confidence level in recommendations.

docs.title: Quick Research
docs.text:
User: /research "Python dataclasses vs pydantic for config"

Agent:
## Research: dataclasses vs pydantic for Configuration

### Summary
Use **pydantic** for configuration with validation, **dataclasses** for simple data containers.

### Comparison

| Feature | dataclasses | pydantic |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| Validation | ❌ Manual | ✅ Built-in |
| Type coercion | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| JSON parsing | ⚠️ Extra code | ✅ Built-in |
| Performance | ✅ Faster | ⚠️ Overhead |
| Dependencies | ✅ stdlib | ❌ External |

### Recommendation
For configuration files: **pydantic** (validation is critical for config)
For internal data: **dataclasses** (simpler, faster)

Confidence: **High** (based on official docs and common practice)

docs.title: Deep Research with Issue Creation
docs.text:

2. Add refresh token rotation
3. Store refresh tokens in httpOnly cookies

---

Create issues for these? [A]ll / [S]elect / [N]one

docs.text:
Extract transcripts from YouTube videos for research and documentation. Uses open-source tools only (yt-dlp, Whisper).

utility · https://github.com/michalparkola/tapestry-skills-for-claude-code/tree/main/youtube-transcript

View on GitHub Download SKILL.md

Extracts transcripts from YouTube videos for research and documentation. Uses a tiered approach: manual subtitles first, then auto-generated, then Whisper transcription as fallback.

docs.title: Extract a video transcript
docs.text: Extract the transcript from this YouTube conference talk: https://youtube.com/watch?v=example

docs.title: Research from video
docs.text: Get the transcript from this tutorial video and create a summary of the key points.

docs.title: Batch extraction
docs.text: Extract transcripts from these 3 YouTube videos for our research project.

docs.title: Credits
docs.text: Based on: https://github.com/michalparkola/tapestry-skills-for-claude-code/tree/main/youtube-transcript

docs.title: Full Specification
docs.text: SKILL.md — Complete technical specification

docs.title: YouTube Transcript Extraction
docs.text: Extract transcripts from YouTube videos for research, documentation, and learning.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Extracting content from tutorial videos for documentation
  • Researching topics covered in conference talks
  • Creating text summaries of video content
  • Building training/reference material from video sources
  • Supporting research with video-based sources

docs.title: Required
docs.text:
• yt-dlp: YouTube video/subtitle downloader (open-source) 
    # Install
pip install yt-dlp
# or
brew install yt-dlp
# or
winget install yt-dlp

docs.title: Optional (for videos without subtitles)
docs.text:
• Whisper: OpenAI's open-source speech-to-text model 
    pip install openai-whisper

docs.title: Transcript Extraction — Tiered Approach
docs.text: Always try the cheapest/fastest method first:

docs.title: Tier 1: Manual Subtitles (Preferred)
docs.text:
Human-written subtitles — highest quality, fastest extraction.

# List available subtitles
yt-dlp --list-subs "VIDEO_URL"

# Download manual subtitles (English)
yt-dlp --write-sub --sub-lang en --skip-download -o "transcript" "VIDEO_URL"

# Convert to plain text
yt-dlp --write-sub --sub-lang en --sub-format vtt --skip-download -o "transcript" "VIDEO_URL"

Post-processing: Strip VTT/SRT timing markers to produce clean text: 

import re

def clean_vtt(vtt_content: str) -> str:
    """Remove VTT formatting, timestamps, and duplicates."""
    lines = vtt_content.split('\n')
    clean_lines = []
    seen = set()
    for line in lines:
        # Skip headers, timestamps, positioning
        if line.startswith('WEBVTT') or '-->' in line or line.strip() == '':
            continue
        if re.match(r'^\d+$', line.strip()):
            continue
        # Remove inline tags
        text = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', line).strip()
        if text and text not in seen:
            seen.add(text)
            clean_lines.append(text)
    return ' '.join(clean_lines)

docs.title: Tier 2: Auto-Generated Subtitles
docs.text:
YouTube's automatic captions — lower quality but widely available.

# Download auto-generated subtitles
yt-dlp --write-auto-sub --sub-lang en --skip-download -o "transcript" "VIDEO_URL"

Note: Auto-generated subtitles often have: - No punctuation - Speaker identification issues - Technical term errors

Apply additional cleanup: 

def improve_auto_transcript(text: str) -> str:
    """Add basic sentence structure to auto-generated transcripts."""
    # Split into rough sentences (every ~15 words or at natural breaks)
    words = text.split()
    sentences = []
    current = []
    for word in words:
        current.append(word)
        if len(current) >= 15 and word.endswith(('.',  '?', '!', ',')):
            sentences.append(' '.join(current))
            current = []
    if current:
        sentences.append(' '.join(current))
    return '\n'.join(sentences)

docs.title: Tier 3: Whisper Transcription (Fallback)
docs.text:
For videos with no subtitles at all — download audio and transcribe locally.

# Download audio only
yt-dlp -x --audio-format mp3 -o "audio.%(ext)s" "VIDEO_URL"

# Transcribe with Whisper
whisper audio.mp3 --model base --output_format txt --language en

Whisper model sizes (trade-off: speed vs accuracy):

Model Size Speed Accuracy Use When tiny 39M Fastest Lower Quick rough transcript base 74M Fast Good Default choice small 244M Medium Better Important content medium 769M Slow High Critical accuracy needed large 1550M Slowest Highest Maximum quality required

docs.title: Step 1: Validate URL
docs.text:
import re

def is_youtube_url(url: str) -> bool:
    """Check if URL is a valid YouTube link."""
    patterns = [
        r'(?:https?://)?(?:www\.)?youtube\.com/watch\?v=[\w-]+',
        r'(?:https?://)?youtu\.be/[\w-]+',
        r'(?:https?://)?(?:www\.)?youtube\.com/embed/[\w-]+',
    ]
    return any(re.match(p, url) for p in patterns)

docs.title: Step 2: Get Video Metadata
docs.text:
# Get title, duration, description
yt-dlp --print title --print duration_string --print description --skip-download "VIDEO_URL"

docs.title: Step 3: Extract Transcript (Tiered)
docs.text:
1. Try manual subtitles (--write-sub)
   ├─ Found → Clean VTT/SRT → Done ✅
   └─ Not found → Continue
2. Try auto-generated subtitles (--write-auto-sub)
   ├─ Found → Clean + improve → Done ✅
   └─ Not found → Continue
3. Download audio + Whisper transcription
   ├─ Whisper available → Transcribe → Done ✅
   └─ Whisper not available → Report: no transcript possible ❌

docs.title: Step 4: Output
docs.text:
Save transcript to project docs:

# Transcript: {Video Title}

- **Source**: {YouTube URL}
- **Duration**: {duration}
- **Extracted**: {date}
- **Method**: {manual-subs | auto-subs | whisper-{model}}

---

{transcript text}

docs.title: Integration with research
docs.text:
When invoked by research, transcripts feed directly into research findings:

## Research: {topic}

### Video Sources
- [{Video Title}]({URL}) — {key takeaways from transcript}

docs.title: Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• DON'T download full video when only transcript is needed
  • DON'T use Whisper when subtitles are available (wasteful)
  • DON'T skip the tiered approach — always try cheapest first
  • DON'T store large audio files in the project — clean up after transcription
